So, here are the types of things. We can have a thing that is replacement. So in a niche, usually, that is a new, seemingly new and different version of the same business or business within a business, an attachment to it, but we're not really taking them out of the niche. So, we're not telling a chiropractor not to be a chiropractor anymore. We're just telling them what you thought was a chiropractic practice, that's not what this is. This bag is a whole different kind of practice. That's new and radical and revolutionary and obsolete everything you ever thought you knew about chiropractic practice. So I wrote a headline the other day for a client. But if everything that is now part of your insert niche business is as obsolete as last year's phone, well, it's an allergy because everybody's got one, right? You've seen that TV commercial now where all the people are POed as they're seeing the new thing right after they bought the thing. It's Best Buy's deal because Best Buy now is this malarkey program where they'll buy back your obsolete thing when you buy the new one so you don't have to worry about buying the new one because it's called the obsolete in a few days. So they show somebody sitting there with their brand new doohickey and there's like a Steve Jobs character speaking to the group of them telling them that now this is version 8.2. But I just bought this yesterday. Yeah, well, so everybody's got this now, right? So I think it's good enough. So replacement is, in most cases, we're not actually taking them out of their business. We just want them to think we're taking them out of their business and everything they don't like about their business. Years ago, I had a guy by the name of Rob Min, he's in a real estate business. And here's how he starts his consulting day with me. I say, well, you know, tell me about your business, where you are, where you want to go? He comes up and got a piece of paper. He says, I got a list of everything I don't like about the business I'm in. I said, oh, great. So tell me the list, right? He said, a real estate business. He said, well, he said, I hate doing listing presentations because, you know, at Best, four or five people, they wind up giving it to their sister in a lawn shed. I hate going to their houses at night. I don't like any part about doing listing presentations, all right? He said, and I don't really, I don't like doing open houses because you got to sit at open houses and you don't know if somebody's got a com or not and a candy in the thing and they sit there all day on a Saturday and my wife bitch is about to week out. I don't like open houses. Okay? I don't like being on call. I don't like people bugging me and the title company calling me that something didn't go through and I got to call them right back. I don't like any of that. I don't like showing houses because it's a pain in the ass. Oh, yeah, okay? Now, he's paying me, you know, at the time, I think my day fee at the time was like 12 Gs so I got to try and keep a straight face for a while. You can laugh. And I won't bore you with the detail but basically when he's done, there's nothing left about the real estate business in his list except getting a check, all right? That's it. Everything else has been eliminated. He don't like any of it, all right? And I'm thinking, well, this could be a short day, you know? But so I say, well, I say, is there anything? And again, I'm trying not to say it in a tone of voice that is, you know, excessively sarcastic. I said, is there anything like you like, you know, other than cash in the checks? Anything at all, you know? And he says, well, I got a few investor clients and I kind of like them because they're not going to be able to sell it to me. They don't care if the countertops are green or blue or they only care about the numbers and they buy multiple properties during the year so I don't have to go through all the work to get somebody and then I can't do any business with them again for five, six, seven years. He's selling multiple properties and I make double money because I make money when I sell to him and I make money when I sell it for him so really everything is twice and pretty much they're pros. They're like business people so they don't bug me a lot, you know? So that's the business. That has to be the business. You're only going to deal with investors and you're going to not do anything else, right, which is exactly now what we did. So everybody's walking around a list of all the stuff they don't like in whatever business they're in. They may not have put it down on paper but believe me, they got it in their head, okay? We all do. You do, okay? I do, okay? The list gets longer by the way with each passing year, you know, as soon as you solve a few and get them off the list another 20, you know, have appeared. My list is different than it was 10 years ago but I got the list, right? And so everybody's got the list and so really the structure of the replacement thing should be designed to wipe out the list. So whoever's in your niche, think about it this way. What's their list? What's their top 10 list that all you're likely to buy prospects would agree on they don't like about the business they're in? Now, obviously some individuals have different things on their list but there's some that are going to be on virtually everybody's list. Now let's make the replacement about not being on the list. So a big replacement that we did in chiropractic in the late 70s to give you an example, so replacement opportunity. At the time 99% of chiropractors were 90% insurance compensated, all right? The entire practice was people were using their insurance. Unless that's from a train wreck which not a lot of offices are located right by the tracks but I did know one chiropractor is right by the tracks and like four times a year there was a big train wreck or somebody's car got hit by a train he couldn't have been happier, you know, it's like oh great. 22 damaged people are all coming in the door. I mean they're literally crawling off the train crawling in the office, sign them up, you know. But you know most people are not in that position, right?
